name: Mehen
description: Mehen, whose name translates to 'coiled one,' is an ancient Egyptian serpent deity. He is primarily depicted as a snake coiled around the sun god Ra during his nightly journey through the underworld, Duat. Mehen's role is to protect Ra from the dangers and monstrous creatures that lurk in the darkness, ensuring the sun's safe passage and rebirth each morning. He acts as a guardian, shielding Ra with his body and warding off threats like Apep, the serpent of chaos. Mehen's coils provide both a physical barrier and a mystical defense, embodying the protective power of snakes in Egyptian belief. While not as widely worshipped as other major deities, Mehen's importance lies in his crucial role in the cosmic cycle of life, death, and rebirth, underpinning the fundamental stability of the universe. His presence highlights the constant battle between order and chaos, with Mehen representing the forces of order and protection. He is a symbol of resilience and the cyclical nature of existence, safeguarding the sun god's journey and guaranteeing the return of light to the world. In some depictions, Mehen is shown as a game played on a board, suggesting a connection to strategy and the challenges faced in the underworld. The game's existence reinforces the idea of navigating a dangerous path with care and foresight, mirroring Ra's perilous journey through the Duat.
subtitle: Serpent Guardian of the Sun God

keyMyths.title: Mehen's Protection
keyMyths.description: Mehen coils around Ra's boat during its nightly journey through the Duat, shielding him from the dangers of the underworld. He protects Ra from the serpent Apep.

keyMyths.title: The Game of Mehen
keyMyths.description: The game of Mehen, named after the deity, is played with pieces moving along a spiral board, representing the journey through the underworld. It symbolizes the challenges and strategies needed to overcome obstacles.

keyMyths.title: Mehen and the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: While not explicitly a judge of the dead, Mehen's presence in the Duat implies his involvement in the afterlife. His protective nature extends to safeguarding the souls of the deceased.

sources.author: R. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Provides an overview of Mehen's role and symbolism.
sources.text: Provides an overview of Mehen's role and symbolism.
